Conseil du ministre
REASONS FOR DECISION
[1] XXXX XXXX (the "appellant"), aka XXXX XXXX XXXX, a national of the United States (US) appeals the negative determination of the Refugee Protection Division (the "RPD") regarding his claim for refugee protection. As is set out in the RPD Notice of Decision dated August 17, 2016, the appellant's claim was heard by the RPD on August 3, 2016 with an oral decision being rendered on that same date.1 The RPD determined that the appellant was neither a Convention refugee2 pursuant to section 96 of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act ("IRPA" or the "Act")3 nor a person in need of protection pursuant to section 97 of the Act.
DETERMINATION
[2] Pursuant to paragraph 111(1)(a) of IRPA, the Refugee Appeal Division (the "RAD") confirms the determination of the RPD that the appellant is neither a Convention refugee nor a person in need of protection. The appeal is dismissed.
BACKGROUND
[3] The appellant was unrepresented before the RPD. On October 13, 2016 a Designated Representative (DR) was appointed to assist the appellant in conducting his appeal before the RAD. The Minister intervened at the RPD but has not done so at the RAD.
[4] No transcript of the RPD hearing has been provided by the appellant. However, a compact disc (CD) of that hearing forms part of the RPD Record (RPDR) and is found on the back page of that Exhibit.4 I have listened to the entire CD and reviewed the RPDR.
[5] The Notice of Appeal was filed on August 24, 2016. Upon the application of the appellant's DR, the RAD extended the time within which the appellant might perfect his Appellant's Record (AR) and deemed it to have been perfected as of October 28, 2016. The AR was accompanied by a collection of extracts from the RPDR primarily consisting of that which was marked as Exhibit 5 at the RPD.5
[6] The appellant does not seek to present new documentary evidence to the RAD pursuant to subsection 110(4) of the Act nor does he seek a hearing before the RAD pursuant to subsection 110(6).6
Basis of Claim
[7] The appellant is XXXX years old. He states that he received a law degree from XXXX and a Masters in economics from XXXX in the 1960s. He was a member of the Bar in New York from 1968 to 1976 and of California from 1966 to 1998.7
[8] The appellant alleges that he has suffered from persecution in the US for a number of years by reason of his political opinion. Broadly stated, he asserts that he has been persecuted and targeted by Zionists and Jewish people in the US due to his political views regarding Zionism and Israel. The appellant states that this persecution and targeting has, inter alia, manifested itself in the last three to four years by his being involuntarily incarcerated on three occasions in the US in jails, psychiatric hospitals and other facilities due to a conspiracy against him and his alleged need of medical/mental treatment. Further details of these assertions will be discussed in the Analysis portion of these reasons.
[9]
The appellant acknowledges that he has a mental illness, specifically XXXX, from which he has suffered since he was a young man.8 A declaration signed by an Officer of the Canadian Border Services Agency (CBSA) on June 20, 2016, shortly after the appellant arrived in Canada and was detained notes that the appellant "was currently taking medication for XXXX XXXX and XXXX XXXX XXXX."9
RPD Decision
[10] The RPD found that the appellant was neither a Convention refugee nor a person in need of protection. In so doing, the RPD found him to be partially credible as follows:10
I found the claimant to be a credible witness in part. I find credible that the claimant was detained three to four occasions and placed in psychiatric sections of the hospitals and the criminal psychiatric wards. I find credible that at each facility there were periodic reviews of the claimant's psychiatric functioning to determine whether he was a danger to his own self or others. In terms of psychiatric hospitals he attended, the claimant was released when those findings were made.
I also find credible that the claimant had periodic administrative reviews of whether he needed to be detained in a criminal part of a jail or in the psychiatric part of the jail. The claimant was released from the psychiatric part of the jail or altogether, when he was assessed to be stable mentally.
[11] However, with respect to the objective basis for the appellant's subjective fear of being victimized by a Jewish or Zionist conspiracy the RPD found:11
The claimant has not presented any credible evidence that Jewish or Zionist conspiracies are the causes of his various detentions. He has not presented any credible evidence that these Jewish or Zionist groups have found him in various locations and these groups have corrupted the judiciary, the police force, the psychiatric hospitals and the conspiracy to silence the claimant.
In fact, the claimant has not been silenced. He remains a vocal proponent of his beliefs and he states his beliefs wherever and whenever he has an opportunity.
[12]
In any event, the determinative issue at the RPD was state protection in that the appellant was found to have not rebutted the presumption of state protection in the US with clear and convincing evidence that the US was unable to adequately protect him. The RPD found that United States is a democratic country with a strong tradition of judicial independence and a history of open and transparent government.
[13] The RPD acknowledged that there are critics of incarceration in the US from ethnic and racial groups but found that the appellant did not fit such a profile. The appellant was held for different reasons, primarily psychiatric. He received treatment, assessment and was then released when he was found to not be a danger to himself or others. There was no evidence that he was mistreated in any way other than being held against his will. The RPD Member noted that, in addition, the appellant has exercised his rights as a citizen to bring his claims to both the Federal Court of Appeal and the District Court thereby demonstrating that the rule of law exists in that country.
GROUNDS OF APPEAL
[14] In his AR, the appellant highlights specific statements in the RPD reasons for its determination which he submits were made in error being based on the Member misconstruing the evidence and, in some respects, arising from the alleged bias of the Member.12 These allegations will be dealt with in greater detail in the Analysis portion of these reasons.
ORDER SOUGHT
[15] The appellant seeks an order that the RAD set aside the determination of the RPD and that the matter be referred to the RPD for redetermination.13
THE ROLE OF THE RAD AND THE CRITERIA FOR INTERVENTION
[16] The appellant made no specific submissions with respect to the role of the RAD in conducting an appeal from the RPD or the standard of review it should apply in doing so.
[17] The RAD has received significant guidance from the Federal Court of Appeal [FCA] in 2016. While dismissing the appeal in Huruglica,14 the FCA provided direction on a number of issues concerning how the RAD is to properly approach an appeal of the RPD's findings of fact, law or mixed fact and law. As stated by Justice Gauthier of that Court:
[69] I now turn to paragraph 111(2)(b). It provides that once an error has been identified (paragraph 111(2)(a)), the RAD may refer the matter back for redetermination with the directions that it considers appropriate only if it is "of the opinion" that it cannot make a decision confirming or setting aside the RPD decision without hearing the evidence presented before the RPD. This possibility acknowledges the fact that in some cases where oral testimony is critical or determinative in the opinion of the RAD, the RAD may not be in a position to confirm or substitute its own determination to that of the RPD.
[70] This also recognizes that there may be cases where the RPD enjoys a meaningful advantage over the RAD in making findings of fact or mixed fact and law, because they require an assessment of the credibility or weight to be given to the oral evidence it hears. It further indicates that although the RAD should sometimes exercise a degree of restraint before substituting its own determination, the issue of whether the circumstances warrant such restraint ought to be addressed on a case-by-case basis. In each case, the RAD ought to determine whether the RPD truly benefited from an advantageous position, and if so, whether the RAD can nevertheless make a final decision in respect of the refugee claim.
[71] One can imagine many possible scenarios. For example, when the RPD finds a witness straightforward and credible, there is no issue of credibility per se. This will also be the case when the RAD is able to reach a conclusion on the claim, relying on the RPD's findings of fact regarding the relative weight of testimonies and their credibility or lack thereof.
[72] Problems will occur when the credibility findings themselves are disputed on appeal, and the RAD has no way to reach a conclusion without endorsing or rejecting those findings. If the RAD can identify an error in situations where, for example, a claimant was not found credible because his story was not plausible based on common sense, the RPD may have no real advantage over the RAD.
[73] Similarly, there may also be cases where a finding that a witness is not credible was based on discrepancies that could not justify such a conclusion or that simply did not exist. If the assessment of the oral evidence contains an error which the RAD can easily identify, but the weight to be given to this testimony is essential to determine whether the RPD decision should be confirmed or set aside, the RAD may conclude that it is a proper case to refer back to the RPD with specific directions in respect of the error identified in the credibility findings.
[74] That said, it is not appropriate to say more about the various scenarios that may arise, for they are not before us. The RAD should be given the opportunity to develop its own jurisprudence in that respect; there is thus no need for me to pigeon-hole the RAD to the level of deference owed in each case.
(RAD emphasis)
[18] Where no issue of credibility is involved, Justice Gauthier in Huruglica FCA found:
[103] I conclude from my statutory analysis that with respect to findings of fact (and mixed fact and law) such as the one involved here, which raised no issue of credibility of oral evidence, the RAD is to review RPD decisions applying the correctness standard. Thus, after carefully considering the RPD decision, the RAD carries out its own analysis of the record to determine whether, as submitted by the appellant, the RPD erred. Having done this, the RAD is to provide a final determination, either by confirming the RPD decision or setting it aside and substituting its own determination of the merits of the refugee claim. It is only when the RAD is of the opinion that it cannot provide such a final determination without hearing the oral evidence presented to the RPD that the matter can be referred back to the RPD for redetermination. No other interpretation of the relevant statutory provisions is reasonable.
(RAD emphasis)
[19] With respect to the scope of the RAD's consideration of the RPD determination, it is not open-ended and is dependent upon the submissions of the parties. As stated in paragraph 103 of Huruglica FCA (see above), the RAD is to carry out its analysis to determine whether, "as submitted by the appellant", the RPD erred. This aspect of Huruglica FCA was subsequently discussed by Justice Gleeson of the Federal Court in Ghauri:15
[30] However, I wish to make the following comments on the RAD's role and obligations and an appellant's onus before the RAD in light of the recent decisions of the Federal Court of Appeal on the RAD. In my view Huruglica at para 103 and Singh v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2016 FCA 96 at paras 54-55 [Singh] underscore the views expressed by Justice René LeBlanc in Dhillon v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2015 FC 321 at paras 18-20 [Dhillon], a case which the RAD relied on when interpreting subsection 110(1) of the IRPA and RAD Rule 3(3)(g) in this case.
[31] Subsection 110(1) of the IRPA provides that a person or the Minister may appeal to the RAD against a decision of the RPD, in accordance with the rules of the [Immigration and Refugee] Board. RAD Rule 3(3)(g) places the onus on the appellant to identify in their Memorandum the errors that are the grounds of the appeal and the location of the errors in the RPD's decision or in the audio or other electronic recording of the RPD hearing.
[32] The Federal Court of Appeal in Singh, in the context of discussing subsection 110(4) of the IRPA, held at para 55 that "These rules must be respected, and it must be presumed that the explicit choices that were made match the objective pursued. It is not the responsibility of the courts to rewrite such provisions when they are intelligible and unequivocal." That principle should equally apply to the appellant's onus to identify the errors of the RPD on appeal as discussed in Dhillon at paras 18-20.
[33] Indeed, the Federal Court of Appeal in defining the RAD's role in Huruglica held at para 103 "Thus, after carefully considering the RPD decision, the RAD carries out its own analysis of the record to determine whether, as submitted by the appellant, the RPD erred." This further reinforces the principle that it is the appellant's responsibility, not the RAD's, to "establish that the RPD erred in a way that justifies the intervention of the RAD. It is not the RAD's function to supplement the weaknesses of an appeal before it" (Dhillon at para 20).
[34] Here the applicants alleged to the RAD that the RPD erred in finding the Taliban were not the agents of persecution, and the RAD could not escape its obligations set out in Huruglica at para 103 by relying on the RPD's IFA finding without more. This was a reviewable error in the circumstances of this case. However, my decision on these facts should not detract from the following principle that emerges from the case-law: appellants before the RAD that fail to specify where and how the RPD erred do so at their peril.
(RAD emphasis)
[20] In this case, the appellant has restricted the scope of his appeal to the issues referred to in the Grounds of Appeal section noted above.
[21] In this case the RPD ultimately determined the claim on the basis of state protection, a question of mixed fact and law. To the extent that state protection in any particular case is assessed in the context of the facts of the matter, the credibility of the claimant gained through the Member's direct questioning of the individual, may enjoy a level of deference from the RAD as discussed above.
[22] Breaches of procedural fairness and the failure of natural justice such as the alleged bias of the decision-maker are reviewable on the correctness standard of review (Canada (Citizenship and Immigration) v Khosa, 2009 SCC 12; Dunsmuir v New Brunswick, 2008 SCC 9.
ANALYSIS OF THE MERITS OF THE APPEAL
[23] The bulk of the documentary material disclosed by the appellant to the RPD consisted of voluminous copies of various court papers prepared by the appellant for use in the US Federal Courts. They number in the hundreds of pages.16 At the end of his questioning by the Member, the appellant was asked if he wished to give any further testimony and he declined stating that it was "all in the 38 pages" that primarily make up what has now been marked as RAD Exhibit 4.17
[24] As I gather from the documents and the appellant's RPD testimony, some years ago he had commenced a lawsuit in the Federal District Court against the US government Medicare/Medicaid programs in order to publicly expose their failures to provide adequate and appropriate care and treatment in their hospitals or facilities.18 Apparently, this lawsuit was dismissed in some fashion due to procedural failures in court filings. The appellant maintains that the merits of the claims have never been adjudicated. The appellant took the matter to the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals in San Francisco. The primary task was to have the appellate court reopen or reinstate the lawsuit that had been dismissed. As well, a significant portion of the appellate filings dealt with the appellant's attempts to obtain in forma pauperis status as he was indigent. A Ninth Circuit Court of Appeal document of May 24, 2016 states that the court had ordered that the appellant pay $505 in filing or docketing fees or file a motion to proceed in forma pauperis, failing which the appeal would be dismissed. The appellant failed to do so within the time permitted and the appeal was dismissed with his application to reinstate the appeal being denied.19
[25] Coincident with the appellant's efforts in the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeal were various events of alleged harassment and improper conduct by court clerks, government lawyers, doctors, nurses, police, law-librarians and many others in authority impeding the appellant and holding him against his will for medical and psychiatric evaluation. He related three specific occasions; the first two being about 3-4 years ago for short periods of time and a more lengthy six-month custodial period from February through August 2015 associated with alleged trespass alleged trespass. He attributes these events to his victimization by Jewish people and Zionists stemming from his political views criticizing Zionists and Israel. Allegations regarding this conduct also found its way into the court pleadings.
[26] The court documents have the appearance of legitimacy in terms of the style of writing, format and having references to, perhaps, hundreds of case precedents. The content of the submissions are highly detailed if, at times, somewhat repetitive, unfocussed and disjointed. By way of illustration, I reproduce the Table of Contents to the appellant's motion "Sanctions for Defendants' Outrageously Unethical Conduct Regarding Plaintiff Surrounding This Litigation20 which he testified told his story:
A. Illegal Removal of Records from Relevant Official Files
B. Illegal Removal of Appellate Case Record from the Ninth Circuit
C. No Need for Pro Se Plaintiff to Use Electronic Means of Filing Documents as Fraudulently Stated by the Circuit Court Clerk
D. The Appellate Court Must Decide A Case Properly Brought Before It Contrary to the Fraudulent Statement of the Circuit Court Clerk
E. Illegal Destruction of Copies of Brief That Were Taken from Plaintiff as Part of Plaintiff's Personal Property at the Time of Plaintiff's Arrest
F. Availability of Jurisdiction for This Brief Provided by the Following:
(1) Sanctions for First Amendment Retaliation and Denial of Access to the Courts Because of Plaintiff's Political Beliefs
(2) Federal Court Jurisdiction over Individuals and/or Entities Because of Their Participation in the Medicare and/or Medicaid Programs
(3) Class of One Equal Protection Jurisdiction Regarding the Removal of Plaintiff's Appellate Record from the Ninth Circuit (the Latter Conspiracy Remedy Brief Section Implies Jurisdiction Because of Conjoint Participation by Federal, and/or Private and/or State Actors)
G. Jewish and Zionist Political Propaganda and Political Offenses
Plaintiffs Opposition to This Propaganda and These Offenses Being the Cause of the First Amendment Retaliation, Denial of Access to the Courts and Class of One Equal Protection Violation Caused by Removal of Plaintiff's Appellate Record from the Court. The Above Illegal Actions All done by Defendants to Plaintiff
H. Threats by Jewish Interests
I. Threats by Jewish Interests to Plaintiff
J. Federal Court Interstate Travel Jurisdiction over Threats to Plaintiff
K. Involuntary Detentions of Plaintiff in Pursuance of an Illegal Conspiracy by Defendants to Violate Plaintiff's First and Fourth Amendment Rights
L. Illegal Failure by Defendants to Provide Plaintiff with Medical and Psychiatric Treatment
M. Illegal First Amendment Retaliation Criminal Prosecution for Trespass to the San Diego County Public Law Library
N. Last Illegal Involuntary Detention of Plaintiff
O. Plaintiff's Illegal Subjection to the Risk of Physical Assault on the Psychiatric Services Unit of the San Diego County Sheriff Central Jail at the Instigation of the Chief Public Defender whose Office was at that Jail Location
P. Illegal Conspiracy to Violate Plaintiff's First Amendment Rights and to Cause Obstruction of Justice (Federal Private and State Individuals and/or Entities Implicated by Such Conspiracy Providing a Jurisdictional Basis for This Motion)
Q. Illegal Harassment by Defendants of Plaintiffs and Individuals Who Aided Plaintiff
R. Punishment to Defendants to be Imposed Because of Their Sanctionable Conduct
S. Violation of Plaintiff's Right of Access to the Courts concerning the Prevention of a Remedy in the Litigation regarding Payment to Hospital Londres of Mexico City for the Emergency Surgical and Medical Services It Rendered to Plaintiff Due Defendant's United States Secretary of Health and Human Services Illegal Refusal to Produce Documents in Response to Plaintiff's Freedom of Information Act Request and Consequent Illegal Failure of the Necessary and Sufficient Evidence to Compel Payment for Hospital Londres' Services
T. Affidavit Supporting the Truth of All of Plaintiff's Factual Contentions in This Brief regarding Sanctions
[27] The court documents disclosed by the appellant also reveal a pattern of the appellant connecting those who have caused him problems with their being Jewish/Zionists. As well, he attributed the results in certain court precedent decisions as being due to the fact that the judges involved were Jewish. Very often he would use the phrase that someone was "apparently" or "evidently" Jewish based upon their surname. The footnote below sets out a non-exhaustive list of examples where these theories were included in his US court proceedings.21
[28] When making his final submissions at the RPD, the appellant summed up what he saw as a pattern of conduct over the years. Whether it was at a library, hospital or even at aXXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX restaurant, people would be nice to him the first day he met them but, if he should return a second day, their attitude would be entirely different, marked by hostility, being banned or with the authorities being contacted.22 He attributed this to Zionists exchanging information about him, thus causing him problems. Similar to the conclusion of the RPD, through my independent review of the evidence I too find that the appellant provided no credible evidence beyond bare assertion and supposition that this had actually, objectively occurred.23
[29] With the above as context, I will address the specific points raised by the appellant in his AR.
1) The appellant alleges that the RPD Member, in referring to him in her reasons24 as a "XXXX XXXX", indicated a "dismissive frame of mind that automatically discredited" his evidence. As well, the appellant writes that "he believes the Member that heard his claim is Jewish and that the Member was biased against him and that this has resulted in an unfair hearing."25 I do not agree with these allegations.
As noted at the outset, the appellant acknowledged that he has been diagnosed with XXXX XXXX As indicated in the CBSA officer's declaration, when taken into custody the appellant had medication for the treatment of XXXX XXXX and XXXX XXXX XXXX. Having listened to the entire CD and reviewed the documents presented by the appellant to the RPD, it is apparent that the appellant's mental problems formed an essential backdrop to his claim and I find that it was correct for the Member to refer to his condition. In fact, I find that she would have been remiss in not referring to that personal characteristic.
With respect to the Member being of the Jewish faith and, allegedly, therefore biased against the appellant, I also find there is no merit to the appellant's claim. I did not note any reference to the Member's religious faith in the CD and it is not clear how the appellant came to his conclusion about it. He did not raise the issue at his RPD hearing nor state in his AR how he came to this knowledge. Having worked with the RPD Member some years ago when I was a part of that Division prior to joining the RAD, I did learn that she has a Jewish background but few details about her specific beliefs.
Again, in reviewing the CD and reading the Member's reasons I could not ascertain that she was anything other than respectful, patient and open to hearing the appellant's evidence. The appellant's assertion that, because the Member is Jewish, she is against him, is certainly consistent with his pattern of associating that faith with his enemies for a number of years as revealed in the court documents and his testimony noted above. It may well be a feature of his XXXX XXXX I find that the linkage made between the Member's religion and the statement that she was biased against him is, as it was in his court pleadings concerning others, an assertion unsupported by objective evidence.
2) The appellant's second allegation of an RPD error is tied to the first in that he links his complaint to the "likely" bias of the Member.26 He alleges that she never understood his position that the persecution he fears is not limited to Zionists and Jews but the fact that the American system is permeated by its position on Jews and Israel which "has created unprecedented access to power for Jews both nationally and internationally...."
While it is true that comments relative to the entire US system being influenced by Zionists and Jewish people are occasionally referenced in the voluminous court filings, the bulk of such assertions are directly tied to the specific acts of members of the Jewish faith who he subjectively believes conspired to do him harm. In my assessment, the Member did appreciate the appellant's point of view and that he believed his political opinions had attracted his persecution. What the Member found, as I have also independently concluded, was that the appellant failed to credibly establish the objective basis for his claim.
3) The appellant's third point is that he believes the Member had not properly "read addressed or rebutted" the particular matters he raised in his US court proceedings dealing with the alleged intentional interference there of his basic rights through the denial of access to material, failure to inform, alleged losses of material and allegedly unlawful detentions etc..27 The appellant submits that the fact that he was always released from these detentions proves that they were illegal. I also disagree with this submission.
Based on the evidence, the handful of times the appellant was detained against his will in the US were primarily related to either his medical or mental conditions which required treatment. The appellant may believe otherwise, but I conclude that it is more likely that these limited instances were motivated by the appellant's need for assistance rather than punishment for his political views. The evidence indicates he was released not because these detentions were illegal but, rather, because the reasons for his being held had ameliorated.
4) The appellant's final two specific points are related to the question of state protection.28 He alleges that his evidence establishes that the US state failed to protect him inasmuch as it perverted the course of justice and that it would be unreasonable to expect him to return to that country to face more of the same. He alleges that the RPD failed to delineate why it found some evidence to be credible (i.e., the dates and times of his various detentions and subsequent releases) while finding other evidence to be not (i.e., the motivation of the protectors of the US system's commitment to the Jewish nation which was manipulated against him). The appellant acknowledges that he had not been silenced from expressing his political views in the US but that it was only through his personal determination stamina and ability that he was able to do so. He now believes that he would not be permitted to speak up "to stop a travesty that is having such a detrimental effect on many in the USA and the Arab world without persecution."
[30] In my opinion, the Member could have correctly ended her analysis of the appellant's claim with what I independently find to be a well-supported determination that, while the appellant had established a subjective fear of the so-called Jewish/Zionist conspiracy against him, he had failed to establish an objective basis for that fear. A finding of persecution requires both the subjective and the objective elements of the claim to be established. The lack of an objective basis for the subjective fear as reflected in the RPD reasons referenced earlier in paragraph 11 of this decision can, and in this case, does, also undercut the credibility of an assertion under section 97 of the IRPA that the appellant had been targeted so as to create a likelihood of the risk to his life, a risk of cruel and unusual treatment or punishment or a danger of torture. If a claim of persecution, risk or danger is not established, it is not typically necessary to consider matters such as an internal flight alternative or the rebuttal of the presumption of state protection.
[31] However, having read the RPD reasons in their entirety, I find that the Member's discussion of the issue of state protection implicitly amounts to an alternative finding to her conclusion that the claim failed because of the lack of evidence concerning an objective basis of a Jewish/Zionist conspiracy directed against the appellant. In other words, while it would of been preferable if she were more explicit, I find that the Member considered state protection on the basis that even if the appellant had established an objective basis for his fear (which the RPD did not find) the claim would still fail due to the appellant's inability to successfully rebut the presumption of state protection in the US. I come to the same conclusion based on my analysis.
[32] As noted by the RPD, the Supreme Court of Canada (SCC) in Ward v. Canada 1993 2 SCR 689 at page 725:
The international community intended that persecuted individuals be required to approach their home state for protection before the responsibility of other states becomes engaged. It is presumed that states can protect their own citizens. As to the complete breakdown of state apparatus, it should be assumed that the state is capable of protecting its own citizens.
[33] At page 724 of Ward, the SCC further found that in order to rebut the presumption there must be clear and convincing evidence of the state's inability to protect.
[34] In dealing specifically with the US, the Federal Court of Appeal in Hinzman29 analyzed state protection in that country concluding:
[46] The United States is a democratic country with a system of checks and balances among its three branches of government, including an independent judiciary and constitutional guarantees of due process. The appellants therefore bear a heavy burden in attempting to rebut the presumption that the United States is capable of protecting them and would be required to prove that they exhausted all the domestic avenues available to them without success before claiming refugee status in Canada. In Minister of Employment and Immigration v. Satiacum (1989), 99 N.R. 171 at page 176 (F.C.A.) ("Satiacum") this Court was called upon to consider a claim of insufficient state protection in the United States and commented on the difficult task facing a claimant attempting to establish a failure of state protection in the United States:
In the case of a nondemocratic State, contrary evidence might be readily forthcoming, but in relation to a democracy like the United States contrary evidence might have to go to the extent of substantially impeaching, for example, the jury selection process in the relevant part of the country, or the independence or fair-mindedness of the judiciary itself.
[35] As noted by the RPD:
In the NDP, National Documentary Package, U.S. Freedom in the World Report of 2016,30 it states that the United States is a democratic country with a federal congress and democratically elected officials in every state. American society has a tradition of intolerance toward government corruption, and the media are aggressive on reporting on such malfeasance. In the U.S. there is a history of open and transparent government. It was the first country in the world to adopt a freedom of information law. The U.S. has a free, diverse and constitutionally protected press. Most importantly, in this case, judicial independence is respected. It is one of the country's strongest democratic institutions.
[36] The RPD does acknowledge that there are critics of the justice system in the US surrounding issues such as the extensive use of plea bargaining in criminal cases with prosecutors threatening harsh sentences to avoid a trial and effectively reducing the role of the judiciary or where municipalities impose fines for minor offenses which can lead to jail terms for those who fail to pay. There is no evidence that the appellant was ever involved in plea-bargaining or received a fine.
[37] There was evidence that on the three to four occasions that the appellant was involuntarily detained against his will for various psychiatric and other reasons he was assessed and released when his condition resolved to the point where he was not a danger to himself or others. This is a form of protection in the US and many other democratic countries for persons with mental disabilities and does not constitute persecution or is considered to be a risk or danger under section 97 of the Act. There are procedures in the US, both judicial and administrative, to ensure that this system of temporary detention is subject to periodic review to prevent its improper implementation or abuse.
[38] With his legal background, the appellant has been able to pursue legal remedies in the Federal Courts of the US to the extent his abilities and means permit him to do. I find that the fact that he has been unsuccessful to date in establishing his claims does not, based on the evidence the appellant has submitted, constitute persecution, risk or danger. I find that the appellant has failed to provide clear and convincing evidence sufficient to satisfy the requirement that he must in order to rebut the presumption of state protection in his country.
[39] Finally, in the summary portion of his submission,31 the appellant notes that he was denied legal aid and lacked funds with which to hire a lawyer. He was unrepresented at the RPD. A Designated Representative (DR) was not appointed at the RPD and the appellant suggests that the effects of his "mental illness, XXXX XXXX may well have interfered in some substantive way" with his RPD hearing.
[40] While a claimant might wish to use the services of a registered consultant or lawyer as counsel at his RPD hearing, such representation is not necessary and the RPD is under no requirement to retain such services on a claimant's behalf. Section 167(1) of the Act stipulates that such representation for a claimant is "at their own expense". The Immigration Refugee Board (IRB) is not associated with the provincial legal aid program and plays no role in its decisions as to whether or not legal services will be provided.
[41] For a claimant who is over 18 years old, section 167(2) of the Act provides that the applicable Division of the IRB is to appoint a DR if, in the opinion of the Division, the claimant is "unable to appreciate the nature of the proceedings". Having reviewed the RPDR, including the Basis of Claim form (BOC) prepared by the appellant and listening to the entirety of the CD, I could not discern that the appellant was unable to appreciate the nature of the proceeding with which he was involved. His replies to questions asked of him were responsive and on point. There was nothing evident to me whereby I could properly conclude that the RPD was in error by not appointing a DR. Accordingly, this ground of appeal is rejected. A DR was appointed at the RAD at the point where it was necessary that the appellant seek an extended time within which he might perfect his AR inasmuch as it was determined that he could use some assistance in complying with the procedural requirements to accomplish that task.
CONCLUSION
[42] For the foregoing reasons, the determination of the RPD that XXXX XXXX XXXXakaXXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX is neither a Convention refugee nor a person in need of protection is confirmed. The appeal is denied.
(signed)
"Philip MacAulay"
November 24, 2016
